coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of full-color carbon dots with N, O surface-state energy-gap-controlled emission
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Beibei
Luo, Wei
Wu, Di
Li, Shugang
Geng, Fang
coredata.dc:description: An easy and effective route for preparing full-color (blue, green, yellow and red) carbon dots (CDs) with brilliant and stable photoluminescent (PL) was developed by using gallic acid and o-Phenylenediamine as precursors. The resulting CDs were then separated by silica-gel column chromatography. The prepared four types of CDs owned high quantum yields (up to 11.2%) and exhibited attractive features such as excellent solubility, high stability, excitation wavelength-independent PL, mono-exponential PL lifetimes, low cell toxicity and homogeneous optical properties. Then, the mechanism of full-color CDs PL was explored. The results suggested that the surface-states of prepared CDs, especially the pyridine N (C = N) and C = O functional groups, were primarily responsible for the full-color PL emission. The blue CDs had the largest content of C = O while the CDs turned to be green as the C = O decreased and the C = N content increased. The surface state regulation of C = N bond was dominant in yellow CDs while red CDs possessed highest content of C = N and new –COOH appeared. The gradual substitution of C = N to C = O made the band gap of CDs narrowed, which is the main reason for the redshift of CDs PL emission peak. Importantly, the easy preparation and unique optical features of these CDs made them potentially useful in numerous applications such as full-color cell labeling, drug delivery and optoelectronic technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Surface state
Photoluminescence
Full-color

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Spectral property of the full-color CDs
PL mechanism of the full-color CDs controlled by surface states

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2022YFD2101001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-d) TEM, HRTEM (inset) images and histograms of the particle size distributions (inset) of b-CDs, g-CDs, y-CDs, and r-CDs, respectively. (e-f) AFM images and height-profiles analysis (inset) along the corresponding lines of b-CDs, g-CDs, y-CDs, and r-CDs, respectively.
FT-IR spectra of the separated CDs fractions (a). Raman spectra of the four selected CDs samples (b).
High resolution C1s, N1s and O1s XPS spectra of (a) b-CDs, (b) g-CDs, (c) y-CDs, and (d) r-CDs.

Images of b-CDs, g-CDs, y-CDs and r-CDs under UV (a) and daylight light (b), respectively. The PL excitation and PL emission spectra of b-CDs, g-CDs, y-CDs and r-CDs at different excitation wavelengths, respectively (e-f).
Model for the tunable PL of CDs with different contents of C = O and pyridine N.
Cytotoxicity of the (a) b-CDs, (b) g-CDs, (c) y-CDs and (d) r-CDs, respectively.

(a) Synthesis scheme used for the preparation of CDs with distinct PL characteristics. (b) Photography of four CDs samples under a 365 nm UV light. (c) The PL emission spectra corresponding to the four CDs samples.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of full-color carbon dots with N, O surface-state energy-gap-controlled emission

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Study, Chengdu University, Chengdu 610106, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Advanced Study, Chengdu University, Chengdu 610106, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Bio-process (Ministry of Education), Key Laboratory for Agricultural Products Processing of Anhui Province, School of Food and Biological Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230601, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Bio-process (Ministry of Education)
Key Laboratory for Agricultural Products Processing of Anhui Province
School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center of Bio-process (Ministry of Education), Key Laboratory for Agricultural Products Processing of Anhui Province, School of Food and Biological Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230601, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An easy and effective route for preparing full-color (blue, green, yellow and red) carbon dots (CDs) with brilliant and stable photoluminescent (PL) was developed by using gallic acid and o-Phenylenediamine as precursors. The resulting CDs were then separated by silica-gel column chromatography. The prepared four types of CDs owned high quantum yields (up to 11.2%) and exhibited attractive features such as excellent solubility, high stability, excitation wavelength-independent PL, mono-exponential PL lifetimes, low cell toxicity and homogeneous optical properties. Then, the mechanism of full-color CDs PL was explored. The results suggested that the surface-states of prepared CDs, especially the pyridine N (C = N) and C = O functional groups, were primarily responsible for the full-color PL emission. The blue CDs had the largest content of C = O while the CDs turned to be green as the C = O decreased and the C = N content increased. The surface state regulation of C = N bond was dominant in yellow CDs while red CDs possessed highest content of C = N and new –COOH appeared. The gradual substitution of C = N to C = O made the band gap of CDs narrowed, which is the main reason for the redshift of CDs PL emission peak. Importantly, the easy preparation and unique optical features of these CDs made them potentially useful in numerous applications such as full-color cell labeling, drug delivery and optoelectronic technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a kind of newly emerged nanomaterial, are composed of internal carbon core with a sp2 domain structure and an amorphous matrix containing abundant functional groups and defects [1–3]. Compared to organic dyes and traditional semiconductor metal quantum dots, CDs exhibit extraordinary features, including outstanding optical qualities, high chemical stability, low cost, low cell toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [4–7]. The photoluminescence (PL) mechanism of CDs is more sophisticated than that of other PL nanomaterials because CDs can be made from a wide range of materials and by many processes, or they can be composed of several intricate structures [8–10].

At present, various CDs have been synthesized and their PL mechanism has also been studied. There are various strategies for preparing CDs with tunable emission, such as increase the size of sp2 domains, enhance the degree of graphitization, change the surface chemistry of CDs with N or O functional group, or construct graphitic N framework. The relationship between CDs size (sp2 domain) and PL redshift has been demonstrated [11,12]. Subsequently, Ding et al synthesized full-color CDs using urea and p-Phenylenediamine as precursors, and they found that the oxidation degree of CDs affected the PL [13]. Han et al further perfected the surface state theory by determining the relationship between the content of C = N and the redshift of CDs PL [14]. Some researchers explained the PL mechanism by studying the content of graphitic N-doping and the degree of graphitization, respectively [15,16]. The current surface state theory, however, was only able to investigate the process of CDs luminescence regulated by a single element.

Here, we report one-pot synthetic route to prepare full-color CDs by self-oxidation and auto-polymerization based on gallic acid and o-Phenylenediamine as raw materials. This method was environmentally friendly, low cost and safe. The prepared CDs with different PL emissions were separated using the polar difference. The detailed characterization and investigation of the full-color CDs indicated that the change in the content of pyridine N and C = O was the key factor that caused CDs PL shift from blue to red. Based on this finding, we proposed a reasonable PL mechanism to explain the effect of surface functional groups on the PL characteristics of full-color CDs.

Gallic acid, o-Phenylenediamine, petroleum ether, ethyl acetate, and ethanol were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. (Beijing, China). Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM, high glucose) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Gibco BRL (Grand Island, USA). 2-(2-Methoxy-4-nitrophenyl)-3-(4-nitro-phenyl)-5-(2,4-disulfophenyl)–2H-tetrazolim sodium salt (CCK-8) was purchased from Zoman Biotechnology Company (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water (Milli-Q water) was used in all experiments.

Morphological and lattice information of CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) taken by a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope from Japan. The AFM images were obtained using a SPM9700 atomic force microscope (Shimadzum, Japan). The FTIR spectra were obtained using a Nexus 470 FTIR spectrometer (NICOLET, USA). The raman spectra were obtained using an XploRA Raman spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 785 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a GENESIS X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (EDAX Inc, USA). The PL spectra were recorded using a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The time-resolved PL spectroscopy was performed using an FLS 920 spectrometer and the UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a Nanodrop 2000C UV–vis spectrophotometer (Thermo scientific, USA).

Firstly, 0.170 g of gallic acid and 0.108 g of o-Phenylenediamine were dissolved in 20 mL ethanol. After ultrasonic treatment for 30 min, the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was purified using a silica column chromatography with petroleum ether in ethyl acetate and ethanol in ethyl acetate solutions as the eluents with gradient elution. The obtained CDs samples with different PL emissions were re-dispersed in water.

The photobleaching ability of CDs was investigated by measuring the change of CDs PL intensity under 100 W UV lamp. The stability of CDs was investigated by placing CDs in solutions with different salt ions concentrations and measuring the CDs PL intensity.

MC3T3-E1 cells at logarithmic growth stage were uniformly inoculated into 96-well cell culture plates. 200 μL DEME medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum, 1% penicillin and 1% streptomycin were then added. The cells were cultured at 37 °C and 5% CO2 saturated humidity for 24 h.

Cytotoxicity of CDs was evaluated by CCK-8 assay. Briefly, MC3T3-E1 cells were separately seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 5 × 104 cells/mL with 10% FBS and 5% CO2. After incubating at 37 °C for 24 h, the culture medium was substituted with DMEM solution (200 μL) containing different concentrations of b-CDs (blue-CDs), g-CDs (green-CDs), y-CDs (yellow-CDs), or r-CDs (red-CDs). Then, the cells were incubated at 37 °C for another 24 h. Subsequently, 100 μL CCK-8 solution was added into each well and incubated for another 4 h. The sample without CDs was used as a control. The absorbance of the solution at 450 nm was measured using a microplate reader (Bio-Tek, America).

The synthesis diagram of panchromatic CDs was shown in Scheme 1
. After ultrasonic treatment of gallic acid and o-Phenylenediamine in ethanol solution, they were heated at 180 ℃ for 10 h (Schemes 1a). Due to the different structure of panchromatic CDs, silica gel column chromatography was used to separate the products. The fractions of CDs mixtures separated on a silica column exhibited blue (450 nm), green (530 nm), yellow (555 nm) and red (604 nm) PL under a 365 nm UV light (Schemes 1b). Compared with the previous work that only one emission wavelength of CDs (520 nm or 556 nm) can be obtained when using a single gallic acid or o-Phenylenediamine as precursor [2,17], our method can prepare the CDs with four emission wavelengths by one-pot step, which demonstrated the high efficiency of our method. The CDs with different PL emissions were named b-CDs (blue-CDs), g-CDs (green-CDs), y-CDs (yellow-CDs) and r-CDs (red-CDs), respectively. It could be seen from the emission spectra that the four CDs with different PL emissions displayed relatively narrow half-peak width and PL emissions occupied the whole visible spectrum region (Scheme 1c).

To obtain the morphology and size of the four kinds of CDs, the particle morphology and particle size distribution of CDs were measured using TEM and HRTEM. The TEM images showed that these CDs were nearly spherical with good dispersion (Fig. 1 a-d). The HRTEM image at 5 nm scale showed that the prepared CDs ranged in size from 2 to 3 nm (insets in Fig. 1a-d). The average diameter of b-CDs, g-CDs, y-CDs, and r-CDs were 2.42 ± 0.42 nm, 2.78 ± 0.56 nm, 2.73 ± 0.47 nm, 2.3 ± 0.39 nm, respectively. The HRTEM images indicated that all the CDs exhibited identical well-resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.22 nm, corresponding to the 100-crystal plane structure of graphene, which indicated that all four CDs contained graphitized cores [18].

Similarly, the AFM images (Fig. 1e-h) also displayed that all the CDs were monodispersed and exhibited similar particle heights of approximately 2–3 nm (insets in Fig. 1e-h). CDs with different PL emission peaks had no obvious difference in the size indicated that the PL emission redshift was not regulated by quantum limiting effect. The PL mechanism dominated by quantum limiting effect mainly showed that the PL redshifts obviously with the increase of particle size, most typical such as semiconductor quantum dots. Therefore, it could speculate that PL behavior of the CDs samples was affected by some other factors other than size.

The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 a) demonstrated that all the prepared CDs contained abundant hydrophilic groups, such as O–H (3425 cm−1), N–H (3200 cm−1), and C-O (1258 cm−1) [4,10,19]. These hydrophilic groups gave CDs the advantages of good hydrophilicity properties and biocompatibility. The absorption band at 2900 cm−1 was attributed to C–H stretching vibrations, while 1600–1700 cm−1 was assigned to C = O stretching vibrations and 1400 cm−1 to C-N stretching vibrations [20–22].

The Raman spectra (Fig. 2b) showed that the D and G peaks were located at 1430 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1, and the ratio of ID/IG increased dramatically from blue to red. It indicated that there may be more defects on the surface of long wavelength CDs.

Furthermore, the elemental content of the four kinds of CDs were analyzed by XPS. According to Fig. S1, the XPS survey scans reveal three characteristic peaks at 285, 400 and 533 eV, corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s on the surfaces, respectively. In fact, the surface of all these samples were only consisted of those three elements (C，N and O). Moreover, the content of C, N and O in CDs with four emission peaks did not change significantly (Table S1) [23,24].

In the high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 3 ), the C 1 s peak of CDs was deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV, 287.8 eV and 289.2 eV, corresponding to sp2 carbons, sp3 carbons, carbonyl carbons and carboxyl carbons, respectively [25]. The N 1 s peaks of CDs at 398.4, 399.1 and 400.2 eV were attributed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphite N, respectively [26]. The O 1 s band could be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.6 eV and 532.9 eV, corresponding to C = O and C-O, respectively [27]. Calculation results showed that the amount of C = O groups decreased as the color of CDs changed from blue to yellow (Table S2 and Table S4). Moreover, the CDs PL changed from blue to red due to the increase of pyridine N group (Table S3), and from yellow to red due to the increase of –COOH (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral property of the full-color CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4 a, the obtained four CDs emit different PL (blue, green, yellow and red) under the illumination of UV lamps. The CDs solutions showed a similar transparent yellow color under the sunlight (Fig. 4b). Like many reported CDs synthesized by aromatics, these CDs obtained also showed excitation independent PL emissions [28]. The PL emission peaks of all the samples (b-CDs, g-CDs, y-CDs and r-CDs) did not shift when excitation wavelengths were changed (Fig. 4c-f). The quantum yields (QYs) for the single fractions after purification in water were 11.2% for b-CDs, 7.8% for g-CDs, 10.5% for y-CDs and 3.3% for r-CDs, respectively.

Temperature and the ratio of reactants composition are critical for the PL intensity and PL color of CDs. The mass ratio between gallic acid and o-Phenylenediamine (Fig. S2a) and the reaction temperature (Fig. S2b) were adjusted in parallel reactions, respectively. The results showed that the highest PL intensity CDs occurred when the mass ratio between gallic acid and o-Phenylenediamine set as 1:2 as well as the temperature fixed at 180 ℃. With the increased of reaction temperature and ration of gallic acid to o-Phenylenediamine, the PL color of the sample tended to red, indicating that the CDs with long wavelength formed. Thus, the careful controlling of hydrothermal reaction parameters including temperature and the ratio of reactants composition gave the prepared CDs with good mono-dispersity, uniform size and high QYs.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the prepared CDs showed a typical π-π* transition in 200–250 nm region, corresponding to C = C and C = N (pyridine N) bonds [29]. However, in low energy region, the absorption bands of these CDs with different PL color displayed distinct absorption bands at 325 nm, 372 nm, 415 nm, and 470 nm, respectively. It indicated that these samples own different surface states (Fig. S3) [30].

The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of these samples demonstrated that these CDs had similar mono-exponential lifetimes of about 2.9, 2.4, 1.3, and 2.14 ns for the b-CDs, g-CDs, y-CDs, and r-CDs, respectively (Fig. S4). The mono-exponential decay characteristics indicated that the excitons were highly stable and the radiative decay was extremely pure with a minimal nonradiative contribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL mechanism of the full-color CDs controlled by surface states
ce:section.ce:para: While the PL mechanisms for CDs remain unclear, the surprising PL of CDs continues to make researchers to carry out deeper studies. There are two popular PL models for CDs: one is related to the band gap transitions in conjugated π-domains, and the other is based on surface defects on the CDs [31]. Besides, some reports attribute the PL redshift in CDs to quantum size effects [11,32–34]. For example, Bao et al [11] found that in addition to the surface oxidation, the size of the CDs size also affected the emission wavelength. This phenomenon of PL redshifts with the nanoparticle size increases is called as the quantum size effect. However, in our study, the TEM and AFM data (Fig. 1) clearly showed that four kinds of CDs with different PL colors possessed similar size distribution, which meaning that the PL emissions were not determined by the quantum size. Therefore, it could be inferred that the PL regulation mechanism of CDs followed the surface state regulation mechanism rather than the PL mechanism dominated by quantum limited effect. The FT-IR and XPS results further confirmed that the four kins of these CDs possessed different surface functional groups. The difference in surface functional groups caused the CDs with different surface-state energy levels and distinct emissive traps, resulting in the CDs fractions with different PL. For example, when the CDs surface was rich in C = O bonds, the n-π* transition of C = O would dominate, while the emission of CDs appeared blue due to the large energy gap of the n-π* transition [35]. Besides, the XPS data displayed that the increased content of C = N and –COOH on the surface of CDs caused CDs PL emission peak redshift, presenting red PL. The blue CDs had the largest content of C = O. As the C = O decreased and the C = N content increased, the color of CDs turned to be green. At this time, the surface state of CDs gradually tended to be regulated by the C = N group, so the PL gradually redshifted. The results were consistent with the findings of Han [36]. For y-CDs, the content of C = O reduced to the lowest, while the content of C = N continued to rise. At this time, the surface state regulation of C = N bond was dominant. Compared with y-CDs, the r-CDs content of C = N increased and the new –COOH appeared. The arrival of –COOH would introduce huge local deformation, resulting in the formation of local states related to –COOH under π*, thus introducing new energy levels and further made CDs PL redshift [37]. Therefore, we deduced that different PL emissions of the CDs were strongly associated with the contents of C = N and C = O functional groups on their surface. When the surface of CDs was occupied by C = O, the energy levels of CDs were mainly contributed by C = O. Thus, the energy gap of CDs was too large to emit long wavelength PL. The increase of pyridine N, the decrease of C = O, and the newly emerged N-groups introduced new energy levels into the electronic structures of the CDs, resulting in more electronic transitions and a red shift of PL. Meanwhile, when surface regulation of C = N bond is dominant, the introduction of –COOH leads to further redshift of emission wavelength (Fig. 5 ). When C = O dominated, the HOMO-LUMO band gap was large, and the CDs emitted blue light, while the CDs surface gradually changed to C = N, the band gap of CDs became smaller step by step, and the emission wavelength was gradually redshifted. Moreover, the structural change caused by –COOH can also reduce the band gap. Based on these results, we assumed that the changes of PL colors were determined by the states of N and O on the surface of CDs.

In order to explore the potential labeling application value of the four kinds of CDs, CCK-8 assay was conducted to evaluate the cytotoxicity of these prepared CDs. MC3T3-E1 cells gave over 85% viability at a high dose of 50 μg/mL, exhibiting low cytotoxicity of the four synthesized CDs (Fig. 6 ).

In order to verify the stability of the prepared full-color CDs, the photostability and the ability resistance to ion interference were studied. As shown in Fig. S5, PL intensity of the four prepared CDs basically remained above 85% after 100 min irradiation by 100 W UV lamp, indicating that the prepared CDs had good photobleaching resistance. As shown in Fig. S6, PL intensity of the four CDs kept more than 90% PL when the concentration of NaCl solution was as high as 2.0 M, which indicated that the prepared full-color CDs had good resistance to salt ion interference. The stability characteristics gave CDs good value in practical application.

In summary, full-color CDs based on gallic acid and o-Phenylenediamine were developed by one-pot method. Four kinds colors of pure CDs (blue, green, yellow and red) were obtained after column chromatography separation and purification. All the prepared CDs showed good stability and biocompatibility. They owned abundant groups (C = O, C = N and COOH–) on the surface, while the difference in groups content determined the emission peak of the full-color CDs. The CDs followed the surface state regulation mechanism, and the change of N and O states on the surface was the main cause that made PL redshift. More importantly, the synthetic and separation procedure established was easy and novel, which offered great application potentials in the field of biology or chemistry.

Beibei Wang: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Wei Luo: Investigation, Methodology. Di Wu: Investigation, Methodology. Shugang Li: Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Fang Geng: Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFD2101001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123117.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Small.

J. Mater. Chem. C.

Spectrochim. Acta Part A.

J Mater Chem B.

Nameghi, et al., Application of green-synthesized carbon dots for imaging of cancerous cell lines and detection of anthraquinone drugs using silica-coated CdTe quantum dots-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor, Spectrochim. Acta A 288 (2023)122200.

Chem. Soc. Rev.

J. Mater. Chem. C

Adv. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphitic nitrogen triggers red fluorescence in carbon dots
ACS Nano
